source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 11.2 Protecting the GRUB2 bootloader (pp. 198-201)

options: To prevent single-user-mode root access, GRUB console access, and booting an insecure OS on a dual-boot system
To speed up the boot process
To encrypt the root file system
To hide the boot menu entirely
questions.why: Without protection, an attacker could boot single-user mode (becoming root), use the GRUB console, or pick an insecure OS that ignores access controls.

questions.q: Generate an encrypted PBKDF2 hash of a GRUB2 password (the command that ONLY produces the hash).
questions.why: grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 prompts for a password and prints its PBKDF2 hash; it does not touch any config file.

questions.q: After grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 gives you the hash, what must you do with it?
options: Manually paste it (with set superusers/password_pbkdf2 lines) into /etc/grub.d/00_header, then run grub2-mkconfig
Nothing - GRUB picks it up automatically
questions.why: grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 requires manual editing: add set superusers and password_pbkdf2 lines to 00_header, then regenerate grub.cfg.

questions.q: Set a GRUB2 password AND update the configuration automatically (the high-automation command).
questions.why: grub2-set-password both sets the password and updates the configuration for you.

questions.q: Which file does grub2-set-password generate automatically?
questions.why: grub2-set-password writes the GRUB2_PASSWORD hash into /boot/grub2/user.cfg.

questions.q: What is the core difference between grub2-set-password and grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2?
options: grub2-set-password sets the password AND updates config (high automation); grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 only generates the hash (manual editing needed)
grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 updates config automatically; grub2-set-password only prints a hash
questions.why: grub2-set-password is fully automatic; grub2-mkpasswd-pbkdf2 just produces a hash you must place by hand.
